rationales.hedonism: He realizes he "hadn't eaten since a cheese sandwich at eleven" and notes even his body and plants "aren't getting my best," showing pleasure and self-care being sacrificed.
rationales.benevolence: He repeatedly prioritizes close others by picking up Lotte's kids, buying them stroopwafels, and making Lotte tea and doing washing before she can protest.

rationales.hedonism: He gives up the Sunday morning he "thought I'd have ... to myself" to handle Lotte's leak and emotional distress, putting his own rest aside.
rationales.benevolence: He cycles over to fix the tap, stays when she starts crying, and wants to "say something better," showing active care for his sister's immediate needs.
He goes back inside, spends the afternoon with Lotte and the kids, and chooses to "carry it" rather than say something that would deepen her fear.

rationales.achievement: He frames the day in performance terms: a "full delivery day," "eight runs," and being "behind schedule" while tracking that the earnings were "decent."
rationales.benevolence: He feels guilty for refusing coffee to an older woman who "looked lonely" and decides he needs to show up for Jeroen because "Jeroen always shows up for me."

rationales.self_direction: He wonders whether this is "something I'm choosing or something that's choosing me" and says putting the wrench down "doesn't feel like an option," signaling a loss of agency.
rationales.hedonism: The role feels increasingly burdensome rather than enjoyable; he describes it as "holding something that keeps getting heavier" and says he does not feel patient or at ease.
rationales.benevolence: He keeps accompanying Lotte through hospital care and says stepping back would leave "a hole," showing sustained commitment to someone close who depends on him.

rationales.hedonism: He explicitly enjoys the night: "Drank too many Jupilers, talked rubbish, laughed until my stomach hurt" and rode home "feeling lighter than I have all week."
rationales.benevolence: He follows through on Jeroen's birthday and notes Jeroen hugged him and was "glad I came," showing effort to support an important friendship.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects his mother's script to "focus on your own life" and insists "I'm not doing this out of obligation," asserting his own chosen standard for how to live.
rationales.hedonism: He describes being chronically depleted: "the tank is always half-empty" and some mornings he sits on the bed before he can stand, showing pleasure and ease are being overridden.
rationales.benevolence: He centers his sister and her children, saying "She's my sister" and "I'd rather be tired than be the kind of person who steps away when things get hard."

rationales.universalism: Seeing "a child no older than ten carrying bricks" while he writes code for "rich hospitals" triggers concern about inequality and people far beyond his immediate circle.
He reflects on privilege and "what survival looks like," connecting the girl's situation to broader structural hardship rather than just his own discomfort.

rationales.self_direction: He independently acts on his convictions by enrolling in "an online course on building accessible software for low-resource settings" rather than staying with the status quo.
rationales.universalism: Sending money to the village school and studying accessibility for "low-resource settings" both aim to help disadvantaged people beyond his immediate relationships.

rationales.self_direction: He resists Raghav's market-value framing and concludes that work "should be more" than money, articulating an independent standard for what his expertise is for.
rationales.universalism: He is drawn to designing for people with "limited internet, older phones, and varying literacy levels" and imagines using his expertise to serve people who "actually need it."

rationales.achievement: He says his identity is "tied to being a working professional" and values having "metrics proving my value" like merged pull requests and performance reviews.

rationales.universalism: He keeps comparing the team's wasted effort to "village school teachers' salaries" and feels each rupee spent on nonsense is lost opportunity for people who need it more.
He reconnects with the idea that "every small action matters" and remembers wanting to build technology that would "make life better," showing concern for broader human welfare.

rationales.self_direction: He proactively asks HR to reduce to "four days a week" so he can redirect his time toward work he finds more meaningful.
rationales.security: He accepts that "the money difference isn't trivial" and still moves toward lower compensation, choosing purpose over financial stability.
rationales.universalism: He wants to teach coding at a community center serving "domestic workers and their children," aiming his effort at people outside his immediate circle who face disadvantage.
He keeps showing up for learners with very little prior access, abandons his planned lesson, and commits to "starting where they are" next week to better serve their needs.
He finds meaning in helping "one person learn to type her own name" and reaffirms that "every small action matters" despite the scale of the world's problems.
